transcript.interviewer: What do people get wrong about you when they first meet you?
transcript.interviewee: They think the steady person they meet now is who I've always been. There's a calm surface, and most people never get past it.
It's layered. Under the calm there's a person who's still a little scared, and under that there's an old version of me I don't show anyone — the one from before everything changed.
I used to be reckless and loud, the opposite of calm. A bad year broke that wide open. So now there's a before-me and an after-me, and the after-me is the one with all these hidden floors.
transcript.interviewer: Is the old you gone, or buried?
transcript.interviewee: Buried, not gone. That's the honest answer. There's a clear transformation — I'm not who I was — but the old self didn't disappear, it just sank down to the bottom layer. You'd have to dig through the after-me to reach the before-me.
transcript.interviewer: So which feels truer: that you've travelled a distance, or that you've got depth? Both, genuinely. I went somewhere — there's a journey — but where I went was down. The trajectory and the depth are the same motion for me. I can't separate them.
